coredata.dc:title: Facile fabrication of cupric ion-carbon quantum dots as tumor-specific nanotheranostics for cuprous ion-mediated chemodynamic therapy and real-time fluorescence imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Li, J.
Liu, P.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CDs) have attracted intense interest in biomedical imaging. However, their very small diameter and non-characteristic fluorescence limit the practical application. Herein, Cu(II)-complexed CDs (Cu(II)-CDs NPs) have been designed via facile complexing CDs with Cu(II) ions, for the tumor intracellular glutathione-activated cuprous ion-mediated chemodynamic therapy and real-time fluorescence imaging. They were quite stable in both neutral and weakly acidic solutions even with lower glutathione (GSH)-level and dilution, emitting very weak fluorescence, while as in the simulated intracellular microenvironment, slight or strong fluorescence was emitted in the normal or tumor cells, respectively. Especially, they could release CDs and Cu(I) ions due to GSH-triggered disintegration. Such features make them promising nanotheranostics in tumor treatment, and the tumor-specific cuprous ion-mediated chemodynamic therapy and real-time fluorescence imaging were revealed in the in vitro cellular tests.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanotheranostics
Tumor-specific
Carbon quantum dots
Copper
Chemodynamic therapy
Fluorescence imaging

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Special Section on "Smart Nanomaterials for Cancer Theranostics”

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and optimization of Cu(II)-CDs NPs
Stability and tumor-specific disintegration of Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province, China (Grant No. 18JR3RA271 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: DLS analysis (a), (b), (c), (d), (e), and (f) and TEM images (g), (h), (i), (j), (k), and (l) of the CDs, Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 in the simulated normal physiological media and Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment for 6 h, 12 h, 24 h, and 48 h. CD, carbon quantum dot; DLS, dynamic light scattered system; TEM, transmission electron microscopy.

UV spectra of CDs and Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 at CDs equivalent concentration of 0.20 mg/mL (a). Inset: dispersions of CDs and Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 under daylight (A and B) and 365 nm UV (C and D); effect of Cu(II)/CDs feeding ratio on the hydrodynamic diameter (b) and Cu content (c) of the Cu(II)-CDs NPs samples; hydrodynamic diameter of Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 over time in different media (d) and after dilution for 100 and 1000 times at 0–48 h in pH 7.4 buffer solution (e). CD, carbon quantum dot; UV, ultraviolet.

(a) PL emission spectra of CDs of 0.015 mg/mL in water solution under different excitation wavelengths. PL emission spectra of CDs and Cu-CDs NPs samples with different feeding ratios of Cu(II)/CDs in pH 7.4 for 48 h (b), pH 7.4 + 10 μM GSH for 48 h (c), pH 5.0 + 10 mM GSH for 6, 12 and 48 h (d), (e) and (f), in pH 5.0 for 48 h (g) at 360 nm excitation wavelength, the statistical results (h) of the fluorescence recovery strength with 0.010 mg/mL, respectively. (i) Digital pictures of CDs (A) and Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 (B) in pH 7.4 for 48 h, Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 in pH 7.4 + 10 μM GSH for 48 h (C), and in pH 5.0 + 10 mM GSH for 6, 12, 48 h (D, E, F) under UV light (365 nm). CD, carbon quantum dot; PL, photoluminescence.

(a) XPS survey spectra of CDs and Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6; (b) XPS core-level Cu 2p spectrum of Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6; (c) XPS survey spectrum of Cu–C-G; (d) XPS core-level Cu 2p spectrum of Cu–C-G. CD, carbon quantum dot; XPS, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy.

UV spectra of MB in different buffer solution without adding dialyzate (a), dialyzates by dialyzing Cu(II)-CDS NPs-6 with different media after adding MB and H2O2 for 4 h (b), dialyzates of pH 5.0 and pH 5.0 + 10 mM GSH after adding MB (A) or MB and H2O2 (B) for 4 h (c), and dialyzate of pH 5.0 + 10 mM GSH after adding MB and H2O2 for different times (d). Insets: digital photos. MB, methylene blue; UV, ultraviolet.

Cell viability assay of L02 and HepG2 cells of CDs (a), Cu(Ac)2 (b), and Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 (c) with different concentrations after incubation for 48 h (∗ denotes significant difference P < 0.05). CD, carbon quantum dot.

Confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) images of the HepG2 cells after incubation with the Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 at 15 μg/mL for 48 h (a), (b), (c), and (d). The labels a, b, and c represent the images of the channels of bright field, 4′, 6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI), and CDs, whereas the d represents the merged image of b and c, respectively.
Preparation and disintegration of Cu(II)-CDs NPs (a) and their theranostic mechanism (b).
Feeding ratio of the Cu(II)-CDs NPs samples.
Surface atomic concentrations from XPS analysis.

head.ce:title: Facile fabrication of cupric ion-carbon quantum dots as tumor-specific nanotheranostics for cuprous ion-mediated chemodynamic therapy and real-time fluorescence imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry and Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry and Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry and Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CDs) have attracted intense interest in biomedical imaging. However, their very small diameter and non-characteristic fluorescence limit the practical application. Herein, Cu(II)-complexed CDs (Cu(II)-CDs NPs) have been designed via facile complexing CDs with Cu(II) ions, for the tumor intracellular glutathione-activated cuprous ion-mediated chemodynamic therapy and real-time fluorescence imaging. They were quite stable in both neutral and weakly acidic solutions even with lower glutathione (GSH)-level and dilution, emitting very weak fluorescence, while as in the simulated intracellular microenvironment, slight or strong fluorescence was emitted in the normal or tumor cells, respectively. Especially, they could release CDs and Cu(I) ions due to GSH-triggered disintegration. Such features make them promising nanotheranostics in tumor treatment, and the tumor-specific cuprous ion-mediated chemodynamic therapy and real-time fluorescence imaging were revealed in the in vitro cellular tests.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CDs) have attracted more and more interest in biomedical imaging, owing to their excellent stability, cytocompatibility, and fluorescence [1]. However, there are still two challenges restricting their practical application. First, their very small diameter makes them rapidly clear from the circulation through extravasation or renal clearance [2]. Therefore, they have been usually used in the form of nanocomposites [3]. Their fluorescence would be heavily diminished due to the static quenching when they were incorporated via covalent bonds from their surface amino or carboxyl groups. On the other hand, the CDs-based self-assemblies via non-covalent interactions were not so stable, especially upon dilution.

Second, the fluorescence of CDs is non-characteristic. That is to say, CDs could emit fluorescence in all biomedical cases. At the best, there is a bit of intensity difference in various biological conditions, such as blood circulation, intercellular or intracellular microenvironments of normal cells and tumor cells. The concentration of CDs also affects their fluorescence intensity; thus, the weak intensity difference could hardly be used as diagnostic evidence. Even with the pH-responsive CDs [4], it is impossible to differentiate the intracellular microenvironment of normal cells and tumor cells because of the similar acidity. The tumor-specific fluorescence is still desired for the tumor diagnosis.

Besides the biomedical imaging application, CDs have been widely used as sensors or probes for heavy metal ions because the fluorescence of CDs could be quenched with various quenching mechanism [5]. The complexation of CDs with heavy metal ions might provide a potential for the ‘turn-on’ fluorescence mode in tumor intracellular microenvironment, by relieving the complexation between the CDs and the variant valence metal ions, triggered by the high GSH-level therein.

Cu is one of the essential trace elements of the human body. As a divalent ion, Cu(II) ion could crosslink the CDs into nanoparticles. Meantime, the complexation could quench the fluorescence of CDs via static quenching mechanisms [6]. The standard reduction potential of Cu(II/I) is 0.153 V. The reduction of Cu(II) to Cu(I) could be induced by GSH, which is over-expressed in the tumor cells. The proposed Cu(II)-complexed carbon quantum dots (Cu(II)-CDs NPs) could GSH-triggered disintegrate in the tumor intracellular microenvironment to release CDs and Cu(I) ions. Because of the much lower quenching effect of Cu(I) than Cu(II) [7], the GSH-triggered disintegration of the Cu(II)-CDs NPs is expected to recover the fluorescence of CDs. Moreover, the released Cu(I) ion could induce OH generation via a Fenton-like reaction, which had been widely used in cancer chemodynamic therapy. Recently, cuprous ion-mediated chemodynamic therapy has been widely reported with the cupric ion-based nanomedicines [8], in the form of Cu(I) [9] or Cu(II) [10] complex, Cu2+-based metal–organic frameworks [11], Cu2+-crosslinked gel [12], Cu-based layered double hydroxide (CuFe-LDH) nanosheets [13], copper peroxide nanodots [14], Cu2S quantum dots [15], copper peroxide nanoparticles [16], and so on. Comparatively speaking, the proposed Cu(II)-CDs NPs could be easily fabricated, killing two birds with one stone.

Based on the hypothesis, the Cu(II)-CDs NPs have been designed in the present work (Scheme 1 A). The tumor-specific disintegration, fluorescence recovery, and Cu(I)-induced ROS generation were revealed. As expected, the tumor-selective chemodynamic therapy and fluorescence imaging were achieved in the in vitro cellular experiments (Scheme 1B).

Citric acid anhydrous (99.5%) was bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ethylenediamine (99%) was purchased from Rionlon Bohua Pharmaceutical Chemical Co. Ltd. Glutathione (GSH, 97%) was obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co. Copper (II) acetate (anhydrous, 98%) was purchased from ALFA AESAR. Methylene blue (MB, 95%) was provided by Tianjin Tiantai Fine Chemicals Co., Ltd. Other reagents were all of analytical grade and used directly without any treatment. Double distilled water was used throughout.
The fluorescent CDs were synthesized via the microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and ethylenediamine, as reported previously [17].

30 mg of CDs were dispersed in 15 mL of deionized water. After dropping 5 mL of Cu(Ac)2 aqueous solution and stirring for 30 min, the Cu(II)-CDs NPs were collected by centrifugation (10 000 rpm for 5 min), subsequently washed with water three times, and finally lyophilization was performed. The Cu(II) (μmol)/CDs (mg) feeding ratios for the samples were summarized in Table 1 .
The morphology and size of the CDs and Cu(II)-CDs NPs were characterized using a JEM-1200EX transmission electron microscopy with samples dispersed in water and deposited on each copper grid.

The hydrodynamic diameters of the CDs and Cu(II)-CDs NPs were recorded with their aqueous dispersions on a dynamic scattered light system (BI-200SM) at room temperature. Each sample was measured three times and the average value was presented.
The ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) spectra of the CDs and Cu(II)-CDs NPs were recorded using a TU-1901 UV–vis spectrometer.
The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted using a PHI-5702 instrument.
The fluorescent emission spectra of the CDs and Cu(II)-CDs NPs were recorded in their aqueous dispersions using a Hitachi F-7500 fluorescence spectrometer.

The photoluminescence quantum yield and time-resolved fluorescence spectra of the CDs and Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 were analyzed using Combined Fluorescence Lifetime & Steady-State Spectrometer (FLS920) and Steady-State & Time-Resolved Fluorescence Spectrofluorometer (FLS920), respectively.
The copper contents in the Cu(II)-CDs NPs were determined on an inductively-coupled plasma-optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES, Plasma Quant PQ9000, Analytik Jena AG, DEU).

The hydrodynamic diameter and fluorescence were recorded after dispersing the Cu(II)-CDs NPs in various simulated media (pH 7.4 PBS, pH 6.8 PBS, pH 5.0 ABS, pH 7.4 PBS + 10 μM GSH, and pH 5.0 ABS + 10 mM GSH) after different times.

50 mg of Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 was dispersed in 20 mL of pH 5.0 ABS containing 10 mM GSH solution. After vigorous stirring for approximately 0.5 h, the product (Cu–C-G) was collected by centrifugation (10 000 rpm for 5 min) and lyophilization for the XPS analysis.

10.0 mL of the dispersion in each buffered solution (pH 7.4, pH 7.4 + 10 μM GSH, pH 5.0, and pH 5.0 + 10 mM GSH) of the Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 (5.0 mg) was dialyzed against 50 mL of the corresponding buffered solution (MW = 500 Da) in an incubator shaker at 37 °C for different times. The dispersion in the dialysis bag was used to investigate the disintegration of the Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6, as well as the fluorescence recovery.

After dialysis for 48 h, the dialyzate was used to investigate the OH generation from the Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 by the MB degradation via Fenton-like reaction [18]. Typically, 300 μg of MB and 20 mL of 30% H2O2 were added to the above dialyzate after 48 h. After further incubation for 4 h, the OH-induced MB degradation was analyzed by measuring the absorbance of MB at 665 nm.
Moreover, the OH-induced MB degradation in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment (pH 5.0 + 10 mM GSH) was recorded at different times.

The 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-y1)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium (MTT) assay was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the Cu(Ac)2, CDs, and Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 on the L02 and HepG2 cells, after co-incubation at 37 °C for 48 h. After 20 μL of MTT (5 mg/mL) solution was added and incubated for another 4 h, the medium was carefully removed, and 150 μL dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added into each well to dissolve crystals. Finally, the cell viability was obtained by measuring the absorbance of each well with enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay appliance at 490 nm.

The cellular uptake of the Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 and subcellular distribution of the released CDs were visualized through fluorescence microscope (DM 4000 B), after incubating HepG2 cells with a dose of 15 μg/mL for 48 h. The cell nuclei were stained with the Hoechst 33 258 and the location of cellular fluorescence was validated with its excitation wavelength of 405 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and optimization of Cu(II)-CDs NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were synthesized via the microwave-assisted pyrolysis method [17], with hydrodynamic diameter around 4.4 nm (Fig. 1 a) and near spherical shape (Fig. 1g). They showed two UV absorptions at 240 nm and 350 nm, attributed to the π-π∗ transition of the C=C bonds and the n-π∗ transition of the C=O bonds, respectively (Fig. 2 a) [19].

Then the Cu(II)-CDs NPs were fabricated via a facile complexation of CDs with Cu(II) ions in water (Scheme 1A), with different Cu(II) (μmol)/CDs (mg) feeding ratios (Table 1). The products showed much bigger diameter than the pristine CDs (Fig. 2b), demonstrating the crosslinking of the CDs with Cu(II) via the complexation effect. And the resultant nanoparticles with bigger diameter are expected to prolong the circulation time by avoiding the rapid renal clearance.

All the resultant Cu(II)-CDs NPs gave a narrow and unimodal diameter distribution (Fig. S1). Moreover, the hydrodynamic diameter decreased with increasing the Cu(II) (μmol)/CDs (mg) feeding ratio from 50:30 to 300:30 due to the increased crosslinking degree, and then remained constant around 150 nm. The results demonstrated that the complexation interaction reached saturation at the Cu(II) (μmol)/CDs (mg) feeding ratio of 300:30.

To further reveal the point, the Cu content in the Cu(II)-CDs NPs samples was determined with the ICP-OES technique. As shown in Fig. 2c, the Cu content in the Cu(II)-CDs NPs samples increased with increasing the Cu(II) (μmol)/CDs (mg) feeding ratio from 50:30 to 300:30, possessing a maximum Cu content of 33.3% in the Cu(II)-CDS NPs-6 sample.

After the complexation, the UV absorption of CDs at 350 nm originated from the n-π∗ transition of the C=O bonds declined heavily (Fig. 2a), revealing the complexation of the CDs with Cu(II) ions from their surface carboxyl groups. Just due to the complexation, the fluorescence of CDs had been quenched (Fig. 2a, inset). The as-prepared CDs exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence with λ max at 360 nm (Fig. 3 a). After complexation with Cu(II) ions, the fluorescent intensity decreased distinctly (Fig. 3b). The absolute photoluminescence quantum yield decreased from 53.06% to 3.42% in pH 7.4 buffer solution under excitation at 370 nm, with similar lifetime of 10.52 ns and 10.48 ns for the CDs and Cu-CDs NPs-6 (Fig. S2).

All the Cu(II)-CDs NPs samples exhibited similar fluorescent property in the simulated normal physiological medium (pH 7.4 PBS + 10 μM GSH) as in pH 7.4 PBS (Fig. 3c), indicating their stability in blood circulation. While as in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment (pH 5.0 + 10 mM GSH), the fluorescent intensity of all Cu(II)-CDs NPs samples increased slightly with prolonging the treating time (Fig. 3 d-f). It could be revealed by the declined diameter after treating with pH 5.0 + 10 mM GSH (Fig. S3). However, the fluorescence intensity only increased slightly even after treating with pH 5.0 for 48 h (Fig. 3g). Such results indicated the GSH-triggered disintegration of the Cu(II)-CDs NPs samples at the high GSH-level media.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and tumor-specific disintegration of Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above analysis of hydrodynamic diameter, Cu content and fluorescent property, the Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 has been optimized for further investigation. The mean hydrodynamic diameter of the Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 was around 152 nm in the simulated normal physiological media (pH 7.4 + 10 μM GSH) (Fig. 1b), similar to those in water (Fig. 2b) and in pH 7.4 PBS (Fig. S4). However, the value decreased slightly with increasing the treating time in pH 7.4 PBS or pH 6.8 PBS (Fig. 2d) due to the ionic strength. While it slightly increased in the simulated normal physiological media, caused by the slight de-crosslinking induced by GSH. Even treating for 48 h, they still exhibited a mean hydrodynamic diameter of <170 nm with a narrow and unimodal diameter distribution (Fig. S4). Besides, the hydrodynamic diameter of the Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 remained unchanged upon extreme dilution (Fig. 2e, Fig. S5). These results indicated the good stability of the Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 during blood circulation (pH 7.4 + 10 μM GSH) and the weak acidic intercellular environment (pH 6.8).

As for the simulated intracellular microenvironment (pH 5.0), the diameter of the Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 became smaller and smaller with prolonging the treating time (Fig. S4), indicating a slight disintegration in such an acidic media. Even though, they only exhibited weak fluorescence after treating for 48 h (Fig. 3g). That is to say, the fluorescence of CDs was still quenched by Cu(II) ions, although some crosslinked structure had been destroyed with H+ ions.

While as in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment (pH 5.0 + 10 mM GSH), the disintegration was obvious (Fig. 1 c-f and i-l) because of the reduction of bivalent Cu(II) ions into monovalent Cu(I) ions. Furthermore, because the Cu(I) ions possess much lower quenching effect than Cu(II) [7], the fluorescence of CDs was recovered (Fig. 3 h and i). These results revealed the GSH-triggered disintegration of the Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment. As a result, Cu(I) ions and CDs were released for chemodynamic therapy and real-time fluorescence imaging, respectively.

To investigate the redox reaction of Cu(II)/Cu(I) induced by GSH, 50 mg of Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 was dispersed in 20 mL of pH 5.0 + 10 mM GSH solution. After vigorous stirring for approximately 0.5 h, the mixture turned from reseda to yellowish-brown (Fig. S6). The product, denoted as Cu–C-G, was collected to analyze the valence state of Cu with XPS technique. Besides the similar N 1s, O 1s, and C 1s peaks with the Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6, the binding energies of Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2 slightly shifted from 931.5 and 951.5 eV to 929.4 and 949.2 eV, respectively (Fig. 4c and d). Most importantly, the original satellite peak at 941.3 eV and 960.1 eV (Fig. 4d) disappeared, proving that Cu (II) ions had been reduced to Cu (I) ions with GSH [18].

The OH generation from the Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 was then revealed by the MB degradation via Fenton-like reaction. As shown in Fig. 5 a, MB was stable in all the four buffer solutions. While adding MB and H2O2 in the dialyzates of the Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 in the above four buffer solutions, the one in pH 5.0 + 10 mM GSH became a colorless solution (Fig. 5b) due to the degradation of MB via the Cu(I)-induced Fenton-like reaction. To further reveal the OH generation from the Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6, MB or MB and H2O2 was added in the dialyzates of pH 5.0 or pH 5.0 + 10 mM GSH. MB did not degrade neither in the dialyzate without GSH nor in the absence of H2O2 (Fig. 5c). The results indicated that the Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 could be triggered by GSH to release Cu(I), which induced the MB degradation via Fenton-like reaction. The color and UV absorption of the solution became weaker over time and almost disappeared after 4 h (Fig. 5d).

The tumor-selective chemodynamic therapy efficacy of the Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 was then assessed via the MTT assays, in comparison with Cu(Ac)2 and CDs. For the normal human liver cell line (L02), no obvious cytotoxicity was found up to 5 μg/mL Cu(II) ion (Fig. 6 a) and 10 μg/mL CDs (Fig. 6b), or both (Fig, 6c), with cell viability >80%. Interestingly, with the same dosage of Cu(II) ions, the cell viability incubated with the Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 were higher than those incubated with Cu(Ac)2. It indicated that the nanoparticles could avoid the cellular cuprotosis caused by the free Cu(II) ions [20].

As for the human hepatoma cell line (HepG2), a dose-dependent cytotoxicity was achieved with the cell viability of 48%, after co-incubation with the Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 at a dosage of 15 μg/mL for 48 h. However, neither CDs nor Cu(Ac)2 produced obvious cytotoxicity to the HepG2 cells. The MTT assays demonstrated the tumor-specific cuprous ion-mediated chemodynamic therapy of the Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6, with anticancer mechanism as following: Cu(I) ions were produced by the tumor-specific GSH-triggered disintegration of the Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6, then they induced the Fenton-like reaction in the tumor cells to produce highly toxic OH, inducing ER stress and mitochondria-mediated apoptosis [21]. Besides, the Cu(II) ions were also generated in the Fenton-like reaction, maybe causing the cuprotosis [20].

The intracellular distribution of the disintegrated products in HepG2 cells was also investigated with the CLSM technique. In the bright field (Fig. 7 a), a large proportion of cell apoptosis occurred after incubation with the Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6 at 15 μg/mL for 48 h [22]. The green fluorescence of CDs was mainly located in the cell nuclei, which were stained with DAPI to emit a blue fluorescence (Fig. 7 b-d). The MTT assays and the CLSM results indicated the potential theranostic application of the Cu(II)-CDs NPs-6.

In summary, Cu(II)-CDs NPs were designed via facile complexing CDs with Cu(II) ions, for the tumor intracellular glutathione-activated cuprous ion-mediated chemodynamic therapy and real-time fluorescence imaging, based on a ‘one stone and two birds’ strategy. By complexing, the blood circulation would be prolonged by avoiding the rapid renal clearance and the fluorescence of CDs was quenched, while the possible cuprotosis induced by the free Cu(II) ions was also inhibited. Triggered by the high GSH-level, the proposed nanotheranostics selectively disintegrated in the tumor cells. As results, Cu(I) ions were generated for the Cu(I)-mediated chemodynamic therapy, while the fluorescence of CDs was recovered for real-time fluorescence imaging, respectively. Such features were revealed by the in vitro cellular tests, making it a promising candidate for future cancer treatment.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province, China (Grant No. 18JR3RA271).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtchem.2022.101040.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots as a new class of nanomedicines: opportunities and challenges
Coord. Chem. Rev.

Chem. Soc. Rev.

Mikrochim. Acta

iScience

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A simple and feasible atom-precise biotinylated Cu(I) complex for tumor-targeted chemodynamic therapy
Chem. Commun.
